Good morning everybody. Good to see you all. Kista, my ever-smiling friend from India. Good to see you. Hello, Akbar Patrick. Paul, Diedrich, Leuk, Marc. Nice to see you all. Julia, welcome. Albert, nice to see you. Happy we are here together for another teaching session in our masterminds. And today we're going to talk about reciprocity. We will dive deep into the principle of reciprocity. And I really like this principle. And the reason why I love this principle is because it works in every culture all over the world. Every culture, science shows us every culture, teach their children from a young age. Somebody gives you something you have to repay. So it's activated in every culture. Like all principles in every language, there's also research about which principles are most powerful depending on age. And what you see is reciprocity is one of the two principles that works also throughout every age group as one of the most powerful principles. And like I said, that had to do with my introduction, because it's deeply rooted in our system. Because when we are young, this is taught to us. When somebody gives you, you have to repay. So, good we're all here. And let's dive into the principle of reciprocity. Martin, good to see you. I see some other people are joining. Thanks for joining. Great you're all here. Anki Taas, nice to see you. Hi, guys. Yes, so let's start talking about reciprocity. And in our practitioner program, we teach a case about a mystery of reciprocity. And I don't teach this case today. I selected another mystery of reciprocity. Of course, we are a US based company and I'm from the Netherlands. And there is such a case available where those worlds combine. So in 2007, was a terrible hurricane named Katrina and it hit New Orleans. And these are pictures of the exact situation. So there was a lot of damage, a lot of terrible things. People didn't have houses, food, anything anymore. And then a politician came to stage, Christian Kroener. And Christian Kroener starts talking, well, everybody, we are so pleased that we, in such a short amount of time, we were able to help so many people. But at that time, 80% of the people in New Orleans didn't receive any help. So what was the situation here? Was there another politician which has nice stories and want to win some souls? No, this was not the case. Actually, Christian Kroener's real name was Christian Kroener. That's a Dutch name. This was somebody from the Netherlands and the Dutch government sent aid sooner to the people than the American government did. Why? Because this happened in 2007. February 1st, 1953, we had a problem, a water flop disaster. It's called in the Netherlands. And the Dutch country is below sea level. So the Dutch broke and almost 50% of the country was under water. And you can now guess who helped us. The people from New Orleans helped us. But there's something interesting. If you look at 2007 and 1953, there's 54 years of difference. So Christian Kroener, he has gray hair here, but he was probably a little child. And as a little child, that he really consciously was aware that people of New Orleans helped the Netherlands. Probably not. So what is the power of reciprocity? And this is a common mistake I like to address first in some detail. Reciprocity is often only perceived as it works on individuals. Reciprocity not only works on individuals. It works on individuals, of course. But it works on organizations. Think about politics. There's a lot of reciprocity. If you approve this law, then the next time I do something for you. If you help me to get the voters here to approve this, the next time I'm helping you. But this also works in business life. If you assign me this project, the next time I hire you for that other project. Between organizations, there's a lot of reciprocity. And as an example, it even works on entire groups and societies. And it stands the test of time. And some people who heard me teaching previously about reciprocity, I always mention this. But in this mastermind, I want to really raise this to awareness because it's often taken as information. But how often we are really conscious of this effect? We can rely on reciprocity and it doesn't only work for individuals. It goes from person to person. Like this example, Christian Kroener never consciously received help. But older generations told him in 1953, people from New Orleans helped us. And when he was in power, he decided we have to help the people of New Orleans and not just help them. The Dutch government decided to help them quicker than their own government of the United States did. So reciprocity can transfer from generations to generations. It can transfer from colleagues to colleagues in the organizational example. And of course, it's individual. But as you all are, you want to get things done. I know a lot of you want to get things done from organizations. Think in that perspective and you can trust on the science of influence by helping somebody in the organization. First of all, they will tell. That has to do with the liking principle because people like to be associated with good news. So you can trust people will tell. As an example, Paul did this wonderful thing for our organization. Not only that one person will know about it. Multiple people in the organization will know about in my example what Paul did. Then the organization now owes and that could be an organization. Paul represents and it could be Paul as an individual. So think in a way if you want to get things done in business life. Don't only think about what can I give that individual. Think how can I help that organization that might work even more powerful because in business, eventually the organization will decide to pay you back. That person who you did the favor to is a representative of that legal vehicle, but the organization eventually will write a check. So my advice here is yes, use reciprocity and think about the individual in the organization. But use your brain power and think one step further. How can I help that organization? That's one level up and we can trust on the power of reciprocity here. That is the first thing I wanted to dive deeper on in this mastermind because I know and I made those similar common mistakes that you are so focused on the individual. That actually you forget that the organization it is that I assume the organization decides to let you train more people. At one moment the individual will grow off the stage, but the organization remains. So ask yourself the question, what can I do for the individual but definitely ask yourself the question as well. What can I do for this organization? And there's a golden rule we teach. That's something else which I would like to address. In our practitioner program, as most of you are aware, we say the golden rule is not this. Give unto others as they have given to you. Why this is not the golden rule? Because now you received first. So reciprocity is based on what I call these emotional bank accounts which has to be in balance. If you have to give unto others as they have given to you, you are paying back. So that means the other one gave first. So this is not the golden rule. We say this is the golden rule. Give unto others as you would have liked them to give to you. Or in simpler words, give back. Oh sorry, no, not give back, sorry. In other words, give first. Give first. But there's something interesting here with give first. Because I like this quote from Thomas Jefferson, the third president of the United States. And he said, there's nothing more unequal than the equal treatment of unequal people. If you think about that more deeply in reciprocity, is you want to give what that person needs. As science tells us, you will get back what you need. So it's not an exchange of gifts. If I give you this pen, the goal is not you give me a pen back. The goal is help people what they need. So therefore it's not a general trick we are doing here with reciprocity. I will dive deeper on which questions you have to ask yourself. And how can we find the answer to those questions later in this mastermind? I want to raise for now top of mind. Don't treat everybody the same. Everybody is an individual. We are all different. And therefore if you want to use reciprocity with the maximum outcome, you should be aware of that. And think what that individual needs. And then that individual will think what you need. And again, not only the individual will do that, that can transfer to organizations and entire groups and societies. So another thing what we teach and I added this in is what is more persuasive, gifts or rewards. And as you know, these are gifts. Rewards work. That's one thing what I want to address. Because sometimes I hear people talking back to me, yeah, you should not give rewards because gifts is more powerful. That's not what we say. Rewards definitely work. And in some cases, you should work with a reward model like, for example, hiring employees. That's almost impossible to do it on the law of reciprocity because there's a business risk. Well, here you have your salary. I hope you come to work next month. That's the risk will become too much. So that's a reward condition. You have to show your work and then you get your salary. Rewards work. It motivates people. Only what science tells us over and over and over again, gifts are more powerful. Only many people mix up gifts and rewards. So in a moment, I will ask you also some questions. First, I want to address this. What is the difference? In our practitioner program, we teach two differences. I want to add a third. And I think that for me, that's the most important insight because that's the effect gift versus reward. So we know a gift you give before and a reward you give after. That's also what he has to do with reciprocity. Then a gift is unconditional. And here many people make a mistake because it's counterintuitive to give something unconditional. We are pre-programmed to think, yes, but if I start giving, I need something back. That's not reciprocity. And already having that mindset, you transfer that language, that mindset into your normal, right? Because, sorry, with your nonverbal cues, with the words you choose, it's clearly you want something back. So language is important in this topic. Unconditional means unconditional. If you frame it, yeah, I give you this. We often do this in real life. Yeah, you have this now for me, this pen. But it would be so nice if you can give me a pencil back in return. We mentioned those comments because it's so counterintuitive for us to give without actually expecting anything in return and just trusting the science. So be aware how you present your gifts because that's a small big. If people feel, even if you give first, but they feel there is a condition, still you don't activate the principle of reciprocity because in their mind, they have a reward condition because they feel how you present it. That there's still a condition. That's one thing I want to raise to awareness, which I know from experience and by knowing from training, seeing outcomes, what they came up with. It's very hard for people to really give something unconditional. And if they do, then it's really hard to present it that is unconditional. So think through how you present your gifts. And now comes my third add on. And that's what does it give. What we teach is in the core motors model, we connect reciprocity to cultivating relationships so it can create a new relationship can improve an existing relationship. What a gift does it opens the relationship. I give you something and it opens the relationship. What does a reward do is it closes the relationship. We don't say it ends the relationship. Of course, the relationship stays in place, but a reward is you get this from me. If you do that, well, that other person does whatever you ask you give the reward and then you're done. So at that moment it closes. If you look at the bigger picture here of how we use these principles, we want to connect reciprocity to create or improve our relationships. And therefore the gift is so powerful it opens. We get first without condition. Now that person feels the pressure of acting reciprocal to us. And that doesn't have to happen in an hour or in a day that can be months, but the relationship remains open. That's what you want to achieve here. Oh, great. Thanks, Luke, for raising your hand. Because I have to say that at the beginning, if you have a question during the session, always feel free to raise your hand. You can do that in the menu tab reactions. There you can raise your hand and then I will do everything I can to answer them as with all the knowledge I have available. Good to see you. Good to see you guys too. The relationship is already open there and a reward doesn't close the relationship, but it closes kind of an interaction. You work for me for a month and I pay you so this interaction is closed. I continue a bit on this topic. So what is not a gift because we confuse that and especially in the online world, we confuse it a lot. So this downloading an ebook, a lot of people think now I give something thinking about gift and rewards. This is a reward. You have to give your email address and then you get something. There's a condition here. So this is not a reward. And I see that people mix this up a lot. People have to give something in return. We do this in our language and we do it all the time online and we think we are giving gifts. We are not. And if you don't give a gift, there is no reciprocity. So then what you do, people start thinking and I hear these, well, reciprocity doesn't work. People give all this value and people don't act on it. No, you asked something already in return, in this case an email address and therefore people think job done. You got what you want. I got my reward. So be aware of how you perceive gifts, perceive them as they are meant to be from the perspective of the science of influence. And then I do this and then I come to you, Martin. And then something I love to address because this is the thing most people don't think about. You can use reciprocity in your copy, in your text and that's very powerful. The award winning study of Dr. Cialdini, what shows this is his hotel study. So you have the social proof test, but you also have the reciprocity one. And there were, again, three different messages. One is like the big hotshots PR agencies came up with, environment is the most persuasive message. Reuse your towels and save the environment. This is by the way also in the practitioner program. Then a reward condition. If you reuse your towels, we will donate for a good cause to save the environment. The partner with us, but this is a reward condition, actually. And then the reciprocity on your behalf, my dear guests, we already donated to a cause to save the environment for you reuse your towels. This is only done in copy, only a small sentence changed here. And some of you know this example because it's like I said in the practitioner program. So I don't talk for hours about this study. By the way, this product, this study won the best scientific study of the year award by Dr. Cialdini. So it's a really highly appreciated study. What fun thing here is this the eye of environmental appeal. We say rewards work, but in this case it didn't. It had a lower reuse of towels and why? Because actually this doesn't feel like a reward. I pay my full rate to stay here for a night and now I cannot even use a nice fluffy towel in the morning. So there is no reward actually. But the reciprocity appeal works the best. Statistically, if you go from 35 to 45, you improve results by 30%, not bad for changing one line of copy. Now, another example you probably didn't hear about organ donor registration. A couple of years ago, there was a lot to do and actually this is an example from the UK. And governments like that people are organ donors because then you can say life. So there was a controversial approach in a lot of governments and also the Dutch government took that approach is. Normally by default, you are not an organ donor and you have to say yes, I become one. They changed it by default. You are an organ donor. Now you have to say you are not. This is based on the principle of active choice. People have to do something actively. Less people will do it. So that increased enormously the amount of donors, but it also backfired because people now you decide as a government what to do with my organs. People don't like that approach, of course. So it was really controversial. So in the UK, they have in the government, the British science team, the British behavioral science team, they start diving into it. So they had a page online where people could opt in and basically the message was please help others become an organ donor. That's basically a reward condition. The reward is the good feeling of helping others. Now they changed this based on reciprocity. If you needed an organ transplant, would you have one? Everybody, of course you would have one. If so, please help others. They didn't even gut the organ yet. They raised to awareness that in the moment they would need it something, they wanted to have it. So basically they felt like they could have it by raising that only that feeling of reciprocation to awareness. That was their hypothesis. Therefore you help others. 2.3 conversion on 3.2. You could analyze this. Well, my story was that 0.9%. It's also 39.1% increase in results. Again, changing a line of copy did the job. And here what I love about this example, it was the feeling of reciprocity. They didn't gut anything. But by raising by what the British science team did brilliant, they rose to awareness. At the moment you would need one, you want to have one. And that subconsciously led to a mindset, to a feeling. Yes, I would take it at that moment. So yes, I want to give it. My suggestion here is we do a lot of things in business. We give a lot of value. Raise that to awareness. In subtle copy. Don't overdo it. Because at the moment then it becomes forceful almost in backfires. Subtle like here. Martin, please. Yeah, thanks very much. Good morning everybody. So it's kind of linked to Mr. Mr.'s question. This is a very famous study. It's in our program so that I don't reach out. There was people had dinner. They got the idea was in the US waitress live from that tip. So would it mint has effect on the tip. In one case, people got one mint. In one case, two minutes. And in one case, one plus one minutes. So the way that came to us one minute on the table with a check and then you walked away. Stopped came back for you nice people and extra mint. If you want to know all the details, this example is also in our practitioner program, but the effect was huge. That's why this study is so famous. You would say, do I give more tip based on a mint? No, rationally, but yes, 3.3. Two minutes. Would I give that double? Definitely no. No, you did four times as much. So why it's meaningful that after dinner you want to have fresh breath and then two minutes. I want super have fresh breath or something. I don't know. But one plus one mint, 20% from 3.3 to 20. That's almost a 600% increase in tip. You live from tips. That's a 600% increase of your net business result. Well, that's not bad for the price of a mint. So why? Because in the last condition, what we say, the gift have to be meaningful, unexpected and customized. And this is what a sleuth and like we teach, we want to be sleuth of influence or in other words, the detective. We have to do our homework. We have to find what is meaningful. How can we give it unexpected? Now can we customize it? And therefore it's for me easy that Martin asked that question because basically we discussed that in a business setting. If you go to somebody, you help them. Now you say, I'm going to think to help you already. Well, to give them just like we said now to give them a tip on influence. That's definitely meaningful. Recording it. It's unexpected because don't promise I record a video now. It's not unexpected. So it loses the power. You see how subtle you need to do things to increase your effect. Just say I'm going to think how I can help you. You come back with a tip, record it. That's unexpected and customized. It was the example. Hey, Pilar, I made this for you. That was the example. Now it's customized. But customized. I want to address one other element. Customized doesn't always have to be on the person. Imagine you are somewhere there are studies done in front of restaurants that people who entered got something. They didn't know their names. It was not customized for that specific individual, but it was handed personal. Dear client, this is for you and they gave it personal. That's also customized. So think about customized in a broader sense because that made your life much easier. You can just give something to somebody you don't know on the street. I like you to have this. It feels for that person now that person receives it. This is really for me that checks the box or customized. The big question is based on the stakes we are in, based on the importance of the influence challenge, we should spend more time, of course, on these three elements. But is it that hard to find nowadays? What I really want you to know and to think about it using reciprocity. Nowadays people tell everything about themselves online. You can find so much information about people. They share everything on Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn. You can easily find what is meaningful for that person. Then you know how to customize it and with a little bit of creativity, you can make it unexpected. Also on business website, more and more businesses have a profile page for each employee. There's a lot of information to be found there. If you tell other people, I like to surprise person XYZ. People always want to help you because they know they will be associated later on with that nice surprise. Because people will tell. Oh, I got this nice surprise from Martin and then information came from Patrick. But oh yeah, I know Martin asked me. Yes, that's the coffee talk. It will come out and people know that. That's why if you say, can you give me advice as we teach? I want to have this nice surprise to their colleagues, to somebody they know. Easy to get that information. But a common mistake not to do it. You don't check what people tell to us, what information they already give to us, what they need at that moment. What is their interest in that moment? That's on social media. We don't ask their colleagues, their friends. But it's a simple thing which only cost you five to max ten minutes and you have this information. And as you saw with the mint, checking one box for meaning foam to three boxes was a 600% increase in results. I don't say it works every time in those huge percentages, but it's worth to invest five to ten minutes to have at least the opportunity to get those results. But that's one thing that I wanted to raise to awareness on this. Then intangible gifts. Some of you who hear me talk about reciprocity, I always address this. There are a lot of gifts you can give that you don't have to go buy something and then give it. Attention, advice, trust, your time and referral, laughter even, a compliment. Also all gifts, if you give them genuinely. Thinking about the example from Martin and Pilar here again, there were numerous intangible gifts. He listened. He built his trust. He shared already some information. Then he took time to think about it. Then he came back with advice, a customized video. There are five, six intangible gifts. It doesn't cost you. From a financial point of view, it costs you nothing. The only thing is, again, be aware of your language. You have to raise them to awareness, especially in pre-sale activities. Yes, really. Stick to the example if you don't mind Martin and Pilar. Pilar, I really listened to you and wow, this is interesting. I'm now going to take some extra time for you. I will think if I can give you an advice. Boom, boom, boom. Three, four intangible gifts, raise to awareness. If you don't do it, it becomes an entitlement. Pilar would think that's normal for Martin. Every time he comes talking, I have ridle of this. That's what he does. That's not the case. Raise them to awareness. And don't again overdo it. Find a subtle way to address these. Using them in your sentences, now you activate reciprocity. So there's a lot to do with reciprocity and language as we are learning here. It's how you write things, how you speak. Subtle sentences that the other person don't even perceive that they are important and the conversations are very important for the science of influence. And that's what I just said. There's also entitlements. And we teach this example and I stick with this because it's such an easy example. Waiting rooms. You always have a coffee machine or a water cooler. But let's imagine we are at the doctor. Is that water cooler? Is that water a gift from the doctor? Definitely not. How can he make it a gift if his assistant would ask, plus you're waiting here. Do you like some water? Now I say yes and they give me a cup of water. Now it's a gift. But what it now became is now the doctor, because he didn't do that and now majority of officers, other waiting rooms, they just have the machines in place. They create their own problem because next time I come, I expect the water or the coffee to be there. It becomes an entitlement. If they remove it, I will be angry. So, I don't perceive it as a gift, but if I cannot have it anymore, I will be angry. We create these situations all the time by our own behavior. We do all kinds of things for other people. And we don't raise to awareness that they are gifts. We don't give the water. We don't give our advice. We just, we, it's just out there. We become entitlements. Please be very careful by doing that because you see the effect. And I think we all been there helping other people. At one moment you say, I can really cannot help you anymore. And that person you helped for so long is suddenly angry. Why? They perceive it as an entitlement. And it happens to you, to me, to everybody. And the thing is because we don't communicate our gifts properly. That's probably the biggest lesson I wanted to address in this mastermind. Go above and beyond on reciprocity. It's much more than exchanging gifts. It's your way of communicating. It's a way how you write things on paper. It's being aware, really being aware of what you already are giving. And use that adequately to your advantage because you are adequately giving it. The only job you have is raise that to awareness you're doing it. So just because something is free doesn't mean it's a gift. I think everybody should write some notes to really master the principle on his desk or sticky note somewhere that reminds you for the next 30 days. Just because something is free doesn't mean it's a gift. And why I say this is to anchor your brain day after day after day. On top of mine, it is a gift what I'm doing. I have to tell people in a subtle way, I have to write it in a subtle way that I'm doing it. And the effect will be enormous. And because of entitlement, that's also why money has such a short-term effect as influence power. It becomes again that entitlement. So where I want to leave for today is the mindset of giving. It's a mindset, how to do this, how to communicate. And I have good news for everybody. Recent research, who is the happiest of both in the exchange? Is it the giver or the receiver? It's the giver. The giver is more happier than the receiver. So you have two big advantages embracing the principle of reciprocity. One, people start paying back to you, want to do things, want to help things, want to get things, your goals to be achieved. And you will be the happiest by doing so. You should almost start feeling guilty, you know all of this. So that's, I think, a deep dive. And the big takeaway for today is I would recommend that post it, no. Because be aware, what are you doing, how are you communicating this? Because that is the small bit for reciprocity. Well, thank you for listening. I see some questions, so happy to answer those, of course. Paul, please. Hi, Paul, thank you. Just two very quick things in the interest of time. On your wonderful example about the organ donation, yes, reciprocity or implied reciprocity, but I also think that it combines brilliantly the principle of consistency and commitment, because actually you're setting them on the path to being an organ donor. And I think it's a very, I think it actually combines two principles really, really well, which is why it's so powerful and why it had such success. And the second thing I wanted to say was... Happy comment, Paul, sorry for interrupting you. Great notice, I want to give my genuine compliment, because that's actually true indeed. And again, it connects principles. You see again a beautiful example how they amplify the effect. Because you commit yourself, yes, I would choose to have an organ if I need one. And also, of course, it does the two things in the, in the two of the three things in the Dr. Nida model, the relationship bit, but it's motivated to go and be a donor. So that's great. Love this. And the second point, which may be helpful to John and indeed any others on this call, that it's something that I love to go down rabbit holes. And whenever I read my Chaldeany books or anything else, the good doctor has given us for free 50 pages of notes and references in this book and 60 pages of notes and references in this book. I would advise anybody if they find an experiment or small, big or something they like to look at the notes, because what Dr. Chaldeany does religiously, scientifically, is give you a really good reference to the experiment, which you can then look up. And just going back to that copyright issue, if I can find an experiment I like or love, I'll try and read the original paper, most of which are online, by the way. And by referencing not just the Chaldeany Institute or Dr. Chaldeany, then referencing the authors of the original paper, it gives it huge authority, another principle. And it encourages people if they want to look it up. I don't think anybody ever has when I've used it, but if you want to look at the original paper, it's here. But the secret I think I want to share with people here is that when I've looked up the original experiments, occasionally you find little nuggets and gold dust that Dr. Chaldeany doesn't have time to write all about in his big book. But there are things in there that you can really bring out an experiment. So I would encourage people to look at the notes section. It's not boring. It's actually some of the best stuff in the book is because the good doctor has put it in there. Brilliant. Thank you, Paul. Love that brilliant comment Paul and indeed great advice. Thanks for sharing. Sebastian. Just one simple question. I love the idea. How would you handle such a kind of let's say toxic person? Yeah, that's always a challenge, of course, because it's you cannot correct the whole world who is on the whole. I would love to because then we would have a much better world, which is a mission for us to get ethical influence out there for not only the reason to influence, but you start recognizing, for example, Sebastian, unactical behavior. If many people would recognize it, unethical persuasion becomes way less powerful than it is today. If I would see that and they would try to persuade me in an unethical way, I would definitely say something because I will tell them yes, my daily job to talk about it. But if it's somebody further, it's not that I see people on the news and I start writing emails and blog posts about those people because then I would have another 12 hour job per day. So, yeah, so I like to correct, but it depends on the situation, of course, and that's basically have to do with manpower. And therefore, I think the mission is more to inform and teach people about what is ethical so they can correct the persons who are unethically trying to influence. Does that make sense to you? Yes.
